<!-- Topic 5: Structural Patterns -->
<!-- Total slides: 4 -->

# Structural Patterns

---

## How does a loop move through every element in a collection? {.smaller}

Structural patterns are about traversal — visiting each element in a vector or array exactly once. The loop structure follows directly from whether you need the index or just the value.

::: notes
Total slides: 4
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Two Ways to Traverse a Collection

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Range-for (value only)**

```{.cpp}
for (int val : scores) {
    cout << val << "\n";
}
```

Clean and safe — no index, no bounds error. Use when you only need the value.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Index loop (when position matters)**

```{.cpp}
for (int i = 0; i < scores.size(); i++) {
    cout << i << ": " << scores[i] << "\n";
}
```

Use when you need the index — to compare adjacent elements, modify in place, or track position.
:::
:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Choosing the Right Traversal

| Need | Use |
|------|-----|
| Read each value | range-for |
| Modify elements in place | index loop |
| Compare adjacent elements | index loop |
| Build a parallel result | index loop |
| Print with position numbers | index loop |

Default to range-for. Switch to index loop when position matters.

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Summary

Structural patterns are traversals. Range-for is the right default — it reads clearly and eliminates bounds errors. Use an index loop only when the position itself is part of the computation.

<!-- Slide 4 -->
